Primary Reason for Home Health/Medical Necessity (POC Element):
The patient is considered homebound because he is unable to leave the home safely or without considerable and taxing effort due to objective mobility, cognitive, and medical limitations
documented during his recent hospitalization. Specifically, the record documents that he ambulates only 3 feet and requires staff assistance; he needs moderate assistance to get in and out of
bed and into the bathroom, moderate assistance with upper-body dressing, maximal assistance with lower-body dressing and bathing, and total assistance with stair climbing (he reports 16
stairs to his living area). He also has notable cognitive impairment and was described as confused with episodes of delirium, making him a poor historian and increasing fall and safety risk with
unassisted community ambulation. Medically, he experienced new-onset atrial fibrillation with a transient rapid ventricular rate (heart rate documented in the 160s on presentation and managed
on telemetry), a recent acute kidney injury with a creatinine peak of 1.63, and ongoing cardiovascular and metabolic comorbidities (history of CABG, hypertension, type 2 diabetes,
hyperlipidemia) that heighten the risk of decompensation with exertion. He is anticoagulated with apixaban (Eliquis), which increases the clinical risk associated with falls. Taken together, these
findings show that leaving the home would require a considerable and taxing effort given his need for close supervision and hands-on assistance for transfers and ADLs, impaired cognition,
recent unstable cardiac rhythm and renal injury, and lack of reliable in-home caregiver support while his wife is hospitalized. Allowable, limited absences are restricted to medically necessary
appointments and brief supervised/community excursions as tolerated (for example, brief supervised walks or attended religious services), with the expectation that any outing will require
accompaniment and direct assistance. These functional deficits, safety concerns, and recent medical instability meet Medicare's homebound criteria and justify the medical necessity for skilled
home health services (skilled nursing, rehabilitative PT/OT, and speech therapy) to monitor cardiac/renal status, provide hands-on training and ADL assistance, reduce fall risk, and promote
Criteria One (One must be met): Describe what assistive devices the patient is dependent on (if any) and specifically why the patient condition warrants the special transportation or the assistance
of another person or describe the condition that makes leaving home medically contraindicated.
Criteria 1 A: Because of illness or injury
Patient needs the aid of supportive devices

List each diagnosis for which the patient is receiving home care in Column 1, and enter its ICD-10-CM code at the level of highest specificity in Column 2 (diagnosis codes only - no surgical or
procedure codes allowed). Diagnoses are listed in the order that best reflects the seriousness of each condition and supports the disciplines and services provided. Rate the degree of symptom
control for each condition in Column 2. ICD-10-CM sequencing requirements must be followed if multiple coding is indicated for any diagnoses.
Code each row according to the following directions for each column:
Column 1: Enter the description of the diagnosis. Sequencing of diagnoses should reflect the seriousness of each condition and support the disciplines and services provided. Column 2: Enter
the ICD-10-CM code for the condition described in Column 1 - no surgical or procedure codes allowed. Codes must be entered at the level of highest specificity and ICD-10-CM coding rules and
sequencing requirements must be followed. Note that external cause codes (ICD-10-CM codes beginning with V, W, X, or Y) may not be reported in M1021 (Primary Diagnosis) but may be
reported in M1023 (Secondary Diagnoses). Also note that when a Z-code is reported in
Column 2: the code for the underlying condition can often be entered in Column 2, as long as it is an active on-going condition impacting home health care. Rate the degree of symptom control
for the condition listed in Column 1. Do not assign a symptom control rating if the diagnosis code is a V, W, X, Y or Z-code. Choose one value that represents the degree of symptom control
appropriate for each diagnosis using the following scale: 0 - Asymptomatic, no treatment needed at this time 1- Symptoms well controlled with current therapy 2- Symptoms controlled with
difficulty, affecting daily functioning; patient needs ongoing monitoring 3 - Symptoms poorly controlled; patient needs frequent adjustment in treatment and dose monitoring 4 - Symptoms poorly
controlled; history of re-hospitalizations Note that the rating for symptom control in Column 2 should not be used to determine the sequencing of the diagnoses listed in Column 1. These are
separate items and sequencing may not coincide.
Z79.84 Long term (current) use of oral

(M1740) Cognitive, behavioral, and psychiatric symptoms that are demonstrated at least once a week (Reported or Observed):
1. Memory deficit: failure to recognize familiar persons/places, inability to recall events of past 24 hours, significant memory loss so that supervision is required
2. Impaired decision-making: failure to perform usual ADLs or IADLs, inability to appropriately stop activities, jeopardizes safety through actions
3. Verbal disruption: yelling, threatening, excessive profanity, sexual references, etc.
4. Physical aggression: aggressive or combative to self and others (for example, hits self, throws objects, punches, dangerous maneuvers with wheelchair or other objects)
5. Disruptive, infantile, or socially inappropriate behavior (excludes verbal actions)
7. None of the above behaviors demonstrated

Ability to see in adequate light (with glasses or other visual appliances)
0. Adequate - sees fine detail, such as regular print in newspapers/books
1. Impaired - sees large print, but not regular print in newspapers/books
2. Moderate impaired - limited vision; not able to see newspaper headlines but can identify objects
3. Highly impaired - object identification in question, but eyes appear to follow objects
4. Severely impaired no vision or sees only light, colors or shapes; eyes do not appear to follow objects
Ability to hear (with hearing aid or hearing appliances if normally used)
0. Adequate - no difficulty in normal conversation, social interaction, listening to TV
1. Minimal difficulty - difficulty in some environments (e.g., when person speaks softly, or setting is noisy)
2. Moderate difficulty - speaker has to increase volume and speak distinctly
3. Highly impaired - absence of useful hearing
(M1400) When is the patient dyspneic or noticeably Short of Breath?
0. Patient is not short of breath
1. When walking more than 20 feet, climbing stairs
2. With moderate exertion (for example, while dressing, using commode or bedpan, walking distances less than 20 feet)
3. With minimal exertion (for example, while eating, talking, or performing other ADLs) or with agitation
SN instructed the patient on energy-conservation techniques, such as taking frequent rests/stops when walking, which can help prevent shortness of breath.

(Excludes Stage 1 pressure injuries and all healed pressure ulcers/injuries)

Ulcer/Injury that is Stageable at DC]
(M1311) Current Number of Unhealed Pressure Ulcers/Injuries at Each Stage:
A1. Stage 2: Partial thickness loss of dermis presenting as a shallow open ulcer with a red or pink wound bed, without slough. May also present as an intact
or open/ruptured blister. Number of Stage 2 pressure ulcers
B1. Stage 3: Full thickness tissue loss. Subcutaneous fat may be visible but bone, tendon, or muscle is not exposed. Slough may be present but does not
obscure the depth of tissue loss. May include undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 3 pressure ulcers
C1. Stage 4: Full thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. Slough or eschar may be present on some parts of the wound bed. Often
includes undermining and tunneling. Number of Stage 4 pressure ulcers
D1. Unstageable: Non-removable dressing/device: Known but not stageable due to removable dressing/device Number of unstageable pressure
E1. Unstageable: Slough and/or eschar: Known but not stageable due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar Number of unstageable
pressure ulcers due to coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar
F1. Unstageable: Deep tissue injury Number of unstageable pressure injuries presenting as deep tissue injury
(M1322) Current Number of Stage 1 Pressure Injuries:
Intact skin with non blanchable redness of a localized area usually over a bony prominence. Darkly pigmented skin may not have a visible blanching; in dark skin tones only it may appear with persis
(M1324) Stage of Most Problematic Unhealed Pressure Ulcer/Injury that is Stageable:
(Excludes pressure ulcer/injury that cannot be staged due to a non removable dressing/device, coverage of wound bed by slough and/or eschar, or deep tissue injury.)
Stage of Most Problematic: Patient has no pressure ulcers or no stageable pressure ulcers

Code the patient's usual performance at SOC/ROC for each activity using the 6-point scale. If activity was not attempted at SOC/ROC, code the reason.
Safety and Quality of Performance - If helper assistance is required because patient's performance is unsafe or of poor quality, score according to amount of assistance provided.
Activities may be completed with or without assistive devices.
06. Independent - Patient completes the activity by him/herself with no assistance from a helper.
05. Setup or clean-up assistance Helper sets up or cleans up; patient completes activity. Helper assists only prior to or following the activity.
Helper provides verbal cues and/or touching/steadying and/or contact guard assistance as patient completes activity. Assistance may be provided throughout the activity or intermittently.
03. Partial/moderate assistance Helper does LESS THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts, holds or supports trunk or limbs, but provides less than half the effort.
02. Substantial/maximal assistance Helper does MORE THAN HALF the effort. Helper lifts or holds trunk or limbs and provides more than half the effort.
01. Dependent - Helper does ALL of the effort. Patient does none of the effort to complete the activity. Or, the assistance of 2 or more helpers is required for the patient to complete the activity.
If activity was not attempted, code reason:
09. Not applicable Not attempted and the patient did not perform this activity prior to the current illness, exacerbation or injury.

PLAN OF CARE ORDERS AND DISCHARGE PLAN
Orders and Treatment (Specify Frequency, Duration and Purpose of Visits): $484.60 (2.iv)
SN Frequency: 1/wk X wk1; 2/wk X wk1;
ADMIT PATIENT TO AVERY HOME HEALTH SERVICES. ALL DIAGNOSIS AND MEDICATIONS VERIFIED WITH THE PRIMARY CARE PHYSICIAN.
The patient is considered homebound because he is unable to leave the home safely or without considerable and taxing effort due to objective mobility, cognitive, and medical limitations
documented during his recent hospitalization. Specifically, the record documents that he ambulates only 3 feet and requires staff assistance; he needs moderate assistance to get in and out of
bed and into the bathroom, moderate assistance with upper-body dressing, maximal assistance with lower-body dressing and bathing, and total assistance with stair climbing (he reports 16
stairs to his living area). He also has notable cognitive impairment and was described as confused with episodes of delirium, making him a poor historian and increasing fall and safety risk with
unassisted community ambulation. Medically, he experienced new-onset atrial fibrillation with a transient rapid ventricular rate (heart rate documented in the 160s on presentation and
managed on telemetry), a recent acute kidney injury with a creatinine peak of 1.63, and ongoing cardiovascular and metabolic comorbidities (history of CABG, hypertension, type 2 diabetes,
hyperlipidemia) that heighten the risk of decompensation with exertion. He is anticoagulated with apixaban (Eliquis), which increases the clinical risk associated with falls. Taken together, these
findings show that leaving the home would require a considerable and taxing effort given his need for close supervision and hands-on assistance for transfers and ADLs, impaired cognition,
recent unstable cardiac rhythm and renal injury, and lack of reliable in-home caregiver support while his wife is hospitalized. Allowable, limited absences are restricted to medically necessary
appointments and brief supervised/community excursions as tolerated (for example, brief supervised walks or attended religious services), with the expectation that any outing will require
accompaniment and direct assistance. These functional deficits, safety concerns, and recent medical instability meet Medicare's homebound criteria and justify the medical necessity for skilled
home health services (skilled nursing, rehabilitative PT/OT, and speech therapy) to monitor cardiac/renal status, provide hands-on training and ADL assistance, reduce fall risk, and promote
HH SN frequency: 1 time a week for 1 week, 2 times a week for 1 week, 1 time a week for 2 weeks for skilled observation and assessment, medication reconciliation, pain assessment, wound
monitoring and management, infection control measures, diet, home safety and disease process education.
HH PT frequency: For evaluation and treatment for mobility, gait, transfer, establish therapeutic exercises, strengthening, home safety and fall prevention techniques training.
-Patient will remain hemodynamically stable with heart rate maintained within physician-defined parameters, demonstrating understanding of atrial fibrillation management and when to report
-Patient will show stable renal function through consistent monitoring of intake/output and lab values, with education provided on hydration and dietary considerations within 4 weeks.
-Patient will safely transfer from bed to chair with moderate assistance and ambulate at least 10 feet with walker and supervision, while demonstrating adherence to fall-prevention strategies
-Patient will demonstrate improved orientation to person, place, and time during nursing visits and verbalize at least two safety precautions to reduce fall risk within 4 weeks.
-Patient (or caregiver, if applicable) will verbalize understanding of anticoagulation therapy (apixaban/Eliquis), including purpose, side effects, and safety precautions to minimize bleeding risk,

Patient JOEL B NEWMAN is an 87-year-old male identified as White. He has been referred for home health care services on April 3, 2026, following his discharge from a short-stay acute
hospital where he was admitted for treatment. The attending physician overseeing his care is Dr. Mark Steinhauer, and the assessment for home health services was completed by Registered
Nurse Emiliya Berberova on April 4, 2026. The patient's preferred language is English, and he resides at home where he is receiving care.
Mr. Newman is considered homebound, meeting the criteria outlined for this status. He has an illness or injury that makes leaving home medically contraindicated and has a normal inability to
leave home due to his condition. Specifically, he struggles with ambulation and endurance, experiences dyspnea with moderate exertion, and feels pain that restricts mobility. As such, leaving
home is a considerable effort for him and often leads to exhaustion, requiring extended rest periods to recover after even minor activities.
The primary reason for Mr. Newman's need for home health services is his diagnosis of G93.41, which indicates metabolic encephalopathy. He also has several comorbid conditions, including
unspecified atrial fibrillation (148.91), type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications (E11.9), essential hypertension (I10), hyperlipidemia (E78.5), and a malignant neoplasm of the colon (C18.9).
These conditions contribute to his overall health challenges and necessitate skilled nursing intervention.
A comprehensive head-to-toe assessment reveals key findings in several areas. Cardiopulmonary evaluation indicates a respiratory rate of 16 breaths per minute, with noticeable shortness of
breath during moderate exertion. Gastrointestinal assessment shows no significant issues, although attention is needed for his dietary restrictions, including a low-salt and low-cholesterol diet.
The integumentary assessment confirms the absence of any pressure ulcers or significant skin issues, and overall safety assessments indicate a need for supervision and assistance with
Psychosocially, Mr. Newman presents with some episodes of depression and feelings of loneliness or isolation, as indicated by a PHQ-2 score of 2. He exhibits impaired decision-making and
some anxiety, particularly in unfamiliar situations, but remains alert and oriented. His cognitive function is generally stable, with occasional forgetfulness. He is able to communicate his needs
and follow instructions, but he may require reminders and support for decision-making.
In terms of vital signs, Mr. Newman's temperature is recorded at 97.4°F, with a pulse of 60 beats per minute and blood pressure of 120/64 mmHg. His blood sugar levels were noted at 125
mg/dL. These vital signs indicate a stable but potentially vulnerable health status requiring monitoring.
Mr. Newman's activities of daily living (ADLs) reflect a significant need for assistance. He can dress himself with some support, bathe with intermittent assistance, and requires supervision for
toileting hygiene and other self-care tasks. His functional ability is categorized as dependent in several areas, particularly in mobility, where he requires a walker and assistance to safely
Mobility assessments indicate that Mr. Newman relies on a two-handed device for ambulation and requires human assistance for negotiating stairs and uneven surfaces. His ability to transfer is
limited, necessitating minimal human assistance or an assistive device. These mobility challenges further emphasize the necessity for home health services to ensure his safety and well-being.
Socially, Mr. Newman lives with another individual who may require additional training to assist with his care. The support network is limited, highlighting the need for structured home health
services to provide both medical and emotional support.
A review of Mr. Newman's medications reveals that he is currently taking five or more medications, including anticoagulants and hypoglycemic agents. It is essential to monitor for potential
adverse effects, drug interactions, and adherence to his prescribed regimen. Education on high-risk medications and their precautions has been provided to both Mr. Newman and his caregiver
to mitigate risks associated with these drugs.
Mr. Newman's primary caregiver is a family member who may need assistance and training to effectively support him in managing his health conditions. This reinforces the importance of
integrating home health services into his care plan to empower the caregiver and ensure Mr. Newman receives appropriate care.
In conclusion, there is a clear medical necessity for skilled services in home health care for Mr. Newman. His complex health conditions, combined with his functional limitations and the need for
assistance with ADLs, necessitate regular nursing visits for monitoring, education, and support. A recommended visit frequency of two to three times per week is essential to address his medical
needs, provide ongoing assessments, and facilitate his recovery and well-being in the home setting.
Patient Care Plan I Goals I Interventions:
Patient's vital signs will be within normal limits.
Check O2 saturation every visit for any shortness of breath on exertion, fatigue/weakness. Check vs every visit and report to PMD if patient vitals are not within the parameters.
Demonstrate pursed-lip breathing exercises, deep breathing exercises, and energy-conserving measures.
Review patient/caregiver on methods to conserve energy, frequent rest periods, sitting instead of standing to perform tasks, and performing slower, purposeful movements which will
limit fatigue. Instruct patient/caregiver on pursed lip breathing exercises, deep breathing exercises to alleviate dyspnea.
Patient will be free from signs/symptoms of cardiac and respiratory distress during the certification period. Verbalize understanding of symptoms of cardiac complications and when to call 911.
Reinforce patient/caregiver on sodium limitations, managing stress, avoiding smoking/alcohol intake, and compliance with prescribed medications to control blood pressure. Encourage
the patient/caregiver to provide a quiet environment, limiting stimuli to promote rest. Advise the patient to get up slowly to offset the feeling of dizziness. Instruct patient/caregiver to notify
SN/MD for signs/symptoms of cardiac and respiratory distress like chest pain, cyanosis, dependent edema, jugular vein distention, palpitations, wheezing, increased respiratory rate, anxiety,
headache, lightheadedness, and shortness of breath.
Patient will be free from cardiopulmonary distress during the care episode. Patient's pulse and blood pressure will be within established parameters.
Reinforce patient/caregiver to check and record blood pressure, including heart rate. Assess cardiopulmonary status every visit.
Improve cognitive function and reduce confusion.
Monitor and adjust medications as needed to minimize side effects impacting cognition.
Educate family members on how to communicate effectively and provide support.
Conduct regular assessments to evaluate cognitive changes and adjust interventions accordingly.
Ensure nutritional needs are met to support metabolic function.
Monitor daily food and fluid intake to ensure adequate nutrition and hydration.
Assess and manage any swallowing difficulties that may affect nutrition.
Provide assistance with meal preparation and feeding as needed.
Enhance mobility and reduce risk of falls.
Monitor patient's mobility progress and adjust interventions as needed.
Maintain a stable heart rate and rhythm.
Administer anticoagulant medications as prescribed and monitor for side effects.
Provide regular check-ins with a healthcare provider to assess treatment effectiveness.
Educate the patient and family about lifestyle changes to support heart health.
Develop a heart-healthy meal plan with a registered dietitian.
Engage the family in discussions about lifestyle changes and support systems.
Problem: Type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications
Maintain blood glucose levels within the target range (70-130 mg/dL before meals)
Provide education on blood glucose monitoring techniques and schedule regular assessments.
Provide medication management and counseling regarding diabetes medications and their effects.
Involve the patient's family in diabetes education sessions to ensure support and understanding.
Achieve and maintain a healthy weight
Monitor weight weekly and adjust the care plan as needed based on progress.
Facilitate regular follow-up appointments to assess weight loss and overall health.
Reduce the risk of diabetes-related complications
Encourage regular ophthalmology and podiatry visits as part of preventive care.
Implement a medication adherence program to ensure proper management of diabetes.
Reduce blood pressure to target levels (e.g., below 130/80 mmHg)
Monitor blood pressure daily and maintain a log for review.
Educate the patient and family on the importance of adherence to prescribed antihypertensive medications.
Schedule regular follow-up appointments to assess blood pressure and medication effectiveness.
Enhance patient and family understanding of hypertension management.
Conduct educational sessions for the patient and family regarding lifestyle changes to manage hypertension.
Involve the family in setting realistic health goals and monitoring progress.
Facilitate communication between family members and healthcare providers to discuss concerns and progress.
Promote medication adherence and management for hypertension.
Review and simplify medication regimens to enhance adherence.
Provide counseling on potential side effects and the importance of reporting them to healthcare providers.
Collaborate with pharmacists for medication synchronization to simplify refills.
Reduce cholesterol levels to within normal range.
Monitor the patient's lipid profile through regular blood tests to track progress.
Provide medication counseling regarding lipid-lowering medications and their importance in managing cholesterol.
Improve understanding of health conditions related to hyperlipidemia.
Conduct educational sessions for the patient and family about the risks associated with hyperlipidemia and the importance of management.
Encourage regular follow-up appointments with healthcare providers to review progress and adjust care plans as needed.
Manage symptoms and improve quality of life.
Provide pain management and symptom relief through medications.
Regular monitoring of vital signs and overall health status.
Ensure nutritional needs are met to support treatment and recovery.
Monitor changes in weight and appetite regularly.
Provide emotional and psychological support to the patient and family.
Incorporate relaxation techniques and stress management strategies.
Conduct regular mental health assessments to monitor emotional well-being.
Reduce the risk of falls in the home environment.
Conduct a home safety assessment to identify potential fall hazards.
Educate the patient and family on fall prevention strategies.
Review and optimize medication management to reduce side effects that may contribute to falls.
Enhance awareness of fall risks and safe practices.
Provide educational materials on fall prevention to the patient and family.
Utilize technology (e.g., alerts, alarms) to enhance monitoring of the patient.
Conduct regular check-ins to assess the patient's understanding of fall prevention strategies.
Verbalize understanding of the importance of pressure-relieving measures.
Instruct patient/caregiver on pressure-relieving measures such as repositioning every 2 hours, float heels, pad bony prominences and methods to reduce friction and shear.
Instruct patient/caregiver to inspect skin daily and notify SN/MD for redness or open sores. Assess skin for breakdown due to risk of pressure ulcer development.
